label: Berlin
sub: Capital of Nazi Germany and seat of Hitler's government. Site of the final Battle of Berlin (April–May 1945) and Hitler's suicide in the Führerbunker on April 30, 1945.

label: Normandy (D-Day)
sub: Site of Operation Overlord, June 6, 1944 — the largest seaborne invasion in history. Allied forces landed on five beaches: Utah, Omaha, Gold, Juno, and Sword, beginning the liberation of Western Europe.

label: Stalingrad (Volgograd)
sub: Scene of WWII's bloodiest battle (August 1942–February 1943). Soviet forces encircled and destroyed the German 6th Army; 800,000+ Axis casualties. Definitive turning point of the Eastern Front.

label: Auschwitz-Birkenau
sub: Largest Nazi concentration and extermination camp complex, in occupied Poland. Over 1.1 million people, predominantly Jews, were murdered here. Liberated by Soviet forces on January 27, 1945.

label: Hiroshima
sub: First city targeted by an atomic bomb, August 6, 1945. 'Little Boy' uranium bomb killed 70,000–80,000 instantly and up to 140,000 by year's end. Hiroshima Peace Memorial now commemorates the victims.

label: Nagasaki
sub: Second city struck by atomic bomb, August 9, 1945. 'Fat Man' plutonium bomb killed 40,000–80,000. The bombing, combined with Soviet entry into the war, precipitated Japan's surrender.

label: Japanese Strike Fleet — Pearl Harbor Departure
sub: Japanese Kido Butai (6 carriers) departed from Hittokapu Bay, Kurile Islands in November 1941 and attacked Pearl Harbor on December 7, killing 2,403 Americans. The attack brought the US into WWII.

label: Leningrad (St. Petersburg)
sub: Besieged by German and Finnish forces for 872 days (September 1941–January 1944). Over 800,000 civilians died, mostly from starvation. The city's defiance became a symbol of Soviet resistance.

label: Dunkirk
sub: Site of Operation Dynamo (May–June 1940): 338,226 Allied troops evacuated across the English Channel after Germany's encirclement. A mix of Royal Navy vessels and civilian 'little ships' rescued the Allied armies.

label: Treblinka Extermination Camp
sub: Second largest Nazi death camp after Auschwitz, in occupied Poland. Approximately 900,000 Jews murdered here between 1942 and 1943. Prisoners revolted in August 1943; the camp was demolished and planted over.

label: Sobibór Extermination Camp
sub: Nazi death camp in eastern Poland where approximately 250,000 Jews were murdered. Prisoner revolt in October 1943 led to the camp's destruction. Site rediscovered through archaeological excavations in 2014–2020.

label: Bełżec Extermination Camp
sub: First purpose-built death camp of Operation Reinhard, in eastern Poland. Approximately 430,000–500,000 Jews were murdered here in 1942. Only two known survivors; the camp was dismantled in 1943.

label: Majdanek Concentration Camp
sub: Nazi concentration and extermination camp on the outskirts of Lublin, Poland. Approximately 60,000 Jews and others murdered here. Liberated by Soviet forces in July 1944 with gas chambers and crematoria still intact.

label: Dachau Concentration Camp
sub: First Nazi concentration camp, opened in 1933. Used primarily for political prisoners and later prisoners-of-war. Approximately 40,000 deaths documented. Liberated by US 7th Army on April 29, 1945.

label: Buchenwald Concentration Camp
sub: Major Nazi concentration camp near Weimar, Germany. Approximately 56,000 deaths among 280,000 prisoners of all nationalities. Liberated by US 3rd Army on April 11, 1945; Eisenhower personally visited.

label: Warsaw
sub: Polish capital occupied by Germany in September 1939. Site of the Warsaw Ghetto Uprising (April 1943) and the Warsaw Uprising (August–October 1944). Germany systematically demolished the city; 85% was destroyed by war's end.

label: Warsaw Ghetto
sub: Largest Jewish ghetto in Nazi-occupied Europe, confining 400,000 Jews in appalling conditions. Warsaw Ghetto Uprising (April 19 – May 16, 1943) was the largest single revolt by Jews during the Holocaust. 13,000 fighters and 50,000+ Jews were killed or deported to Treblinka.

label: El Alamein, Egypt
sub: Site of two decisive battles in North Africa (July and October–November 1942). Montgomery's Eighth Army decisively defeated Rommel's Afrika Korps, turning the tide in North Africa. Called 'the end of the beginning' by Churchill.

label: Kursk
sub: Site of the largest tank battle in history (July 1943). German Operation Citadel failed to eliminate the Soviet salient; Red Army's counter-offensive permanently seized the strategic initiative on the Eastern Front.

label: Midway Battle Area (Central Pacific)
sub: The June 4–7, 1942 Battle of Midway saw US carrier aircraft sink four Japanese fleet carriers — the core of Japan's striking power — reversing the Pacific War's momentum. US codebreakers (Op-20-G) cracked the Japanese naval code, enabling the ambush.

label: Guadalcanal
sub: Site of the first US offensive ground operation in the Pacific (August 1942–February 1943). Six months of intense fighting in the jungles and surrounding seas ended in Japan's first major land defeat of the war.

label: Iwo Jima
sub: Heavily fortified volcanic island captured by US Marines (February–March 1945) in one of WWII's bloodiest Pacific battles. The flag-raising on Mount Suribachi became an iconic image. Nearly all 21,000 Japanese defenders died.

label: Okinawa
sub: Site of WWII's last and bloodiest Pacific land battle (April–June 1945). Involved 287,000 US troops versus 100,000 Japanese defenders. Combined US, Japanese, and Okinawan civilian deaths exceeded 200,000. Kamikaze attacks sank 36 ships.

label: Tokyo
sub: Japanese capital and center of Imperial war command. Subject to massive B-29 firebombing raids from March 1945. Operation Meetinghouse (March 9–10, 1945) killed an estimated 80,000–100,000 civilians in a single night.

label: London
sub: British capital and center of Allied command-in-exile. Suffered the Blitz (1940–41) with nightly German bombing raids killing 43,000 civilians. Churchill's War Cabinet coordinated Allied strategy from underground bunkers.

label: Moscow
sub: Soviet capital nearly reached by German forces in December 1941 (Battle of Moscow). Soviet counter-offensive under Zhukov halted Germany's advance 20 km from the city. Moscow's defense was the first major German defeat of the war.

label: Rome
sub: Italian capital declared an 'open city' by Germans in 1943 to spare it from Allied bombing. Liberated by US 5th Army on June 4, 1944 — the first Axis capital to fall. Mussolini's former seat of power at the Palazzo Venezia.

label: Paris
sub: French capital occupied by Germany from June 14, 1940 to August 25, 1944 — 1,533 days. German commander von Choltitz defied Hitler's order to destroy Paris before surrendering to Free French forces. Liberation celebrations on the Champs-Élysées drew millions.

label: Nuremberg
sub: Former site of Nazi Party rallies and symbol of the Third Reich. Site of the Nuremberg Laws (1935) stripping Jews of citizenship. Chosen for the post-war international war crimes tribunal (1945–46) as a symbolic location.

label: Coventry
sub: British industrial city devastated by Luftwaffe firebombing on November 14, 1940 (Operation Moonlight Sonata). The medieval cathedral was destroyed; 568 civilians killed. Churchill refused to warn the city to protect the Ultra intelligence secret.

label: Hamburg
sub: German port city devastated by RAF Operation Gomorrah firebombing raids (July–August 1943). The firestorm killed approximately 37,000 civilians and destroyed 280,000 dwellings. Germany's second city was effectively destroyed.

label: Dresden
sub: German cultural city firebombed by British and US aircraft (February 13–15, 1945). Official German commission (2010) confirmed 22,700–25,000 deaths. The bombing's military necessity remains debated; the city center was largely destroyed.

label: Arnhem, Netherlands
sub: Site of Operation Market Garden (September 1944): British 1st Airborne Division's doomed battle at 'A Bridge Too Far.' Only 2,400 of 10,000 British paratroopers escaped. The operation's failure prolonged the war in Western Europe.

label: Bastogne, Belgium
sub: Site of the heroic US defense during the Battle of the Bulge (December 1944). Surrounded 101st Airborne Division under General McAuliffe famously replied 'Nuts!' to German surrender demands. Patton's 3rd Army broke the siege on December 26.

label: Remagen Bridge, Rhine
sub: US forces captured the Ludendorff railway bridge at Remagen on March 7, 1945 — the first Allied crossing of the Rhine in Germany since Napoleon. Eisenhower called it the greatest windfall of the war; American forces flooded across for ten days before the bridge collapsed.

label: Monte Cassino, Italy
sub: Site of four brutal Allied assaults on Germany's Winter Line (Gustav Line) between January and May 1944. The historic Benedictine monastery was controversially bombed. Cassino was finally captured by Free Polish forces on May 18, 1944.

label: Anzio, Italy
sub: Allied amphibious landing (Operation Shingle, January 22, 1944) designed to outflank the Gustav Line. The beachhead quickly bogged down; Allied forces endured four months under German fire before breaking out in May 1944.

label: Tobruk, Libya
sub: Strategic North African port besieged by Axis forces for 241 days (April–November 1941) and held by Australian troops. Fell to Rommel in June 1942 after a second siege, shocking Churchill. Recaptured by Eighth Army in November 1942.

label: Palermo, Sicily
sub: Capital of Sicily, captured by Patton's 7th Army on July 22, 1943 during Operation Husky. Patton's rapid advance through western Sicily in a race with Montgomery demonstrated American offensive capability and established him as a front-line commander.

label: Bletchley Park, England
sub: British codebreaking center where Alan Turing and thousands of analysts cracked German Enigma and Lorenz cipher machines (Ultra intelligence). Churchill called Bletchley's work the 'geese that laid the golden eggs but never cackled.' Estimated to have shortened the war by two to four years.

label: Yalta, Crimea
sub: Site of the Yalta Conference (February 4–11, 1945) where Roosevelt, Churchill, and Stalin shaped the post-war world order. Agreements on German occupation zones, Poland's borders, and Soviet entry into the Pacific War were reached at Livadia Palace.

label: Potsdam, Germany
sub: Site of the final Allied conference (July 17–August 2, 1945) at Cecilienhof Palace. Truman, Churchill/Attlee, and Stalin finalized German occupation and issued the Potsdam Declaration demanding Japan's unconditional surrender.

label: Katyn Forest, Russia
sub: Site where NKVD agents executed approximately 8,000 Polish military officers (April–May 1940) as part of the broader Katyn massacre of ~22,000 Poles. Discovered by Germany in 1943; Soviet responsibility denied until 1990.

label: Warsaw Uprising 1944
sub: Polish Home Army (AK) rose against German occupation on August 1, 1944, expecting Soviet relief. Stalin halted Soviet advances. Germany crushed the uprising in 63 days; 200,000 civilians were killed and Warsaw was systematically destroyed.

label: Leyte Gulf, Philippines
sub: Site of the largest naval battle in history (October 23–26, 1944). The US Navy decisively defeated the Imperial Japanese Navy, losing 3 ships but sinking 26 Japanese vessels. Japan's navy never recovered and resorted to kamikaze tactics.

label: Tarawa Atoll
sub: Site of one of the Pacific War's bloodiest battles (November 1943). US Marines stormed the heavily fortified Betio island; 1,000 Marines died in 76 hours. Almost all 4,836 Japanese defenders died. The battle shocked the American public.

label: Saipan, Mariana Islands
sub: Japanese-held island captured by US forces (June–July 1944). Loss of Saipan brought Japan within B-29 range and prompted Prime Minister Tojo's resignation. Thousands of Japanese civilians committed suicide rather than surrender.

label: Singapore
sub: British 'impregnable fortress' captured by Japanese forces on February 15, 1942. General Percival surrendered 85,000 Allied troops — Britain's worst military defeat. Churchill called it 'the worst disaster and largest capitulation in British history.'

label: Rangoon (Yangon), Burma
sub: Capital of Burma, captured by Japan in March 1942, closing the Burma Road supply route to China. Allied forces under Slim's 14th Army, including Indian, British, and African troops, fought the grueling Burma campaign to retake it, liberated May 1945.

label: Cherbourg, France
sub: Major French port captured by US forces on June 26, 1944, three weeks after D-Day. Cherbourg's deep-water port was vital for Allied logistics; Germans destroyed port facilities before surrendering. Repairs took months to complete.

label: Reims, France — German Surrender
sub: Site where Germany signed the first act of military surrender on May 7, 1945 at SHAEF headquarters. General Jodl signed for Germany; General Bedell Smith for the Allies. The formal ceremony was repeated in Berlin the following day.

label: USS Missouri — Tokyo Bay
sub: Site of Japan's formal surrender ceremony on September 2, 1945 aboard the battleship USS Missouri. General MacArthur presided; Emperor's representatives signed the surrender documents, ending World War II.

label: Volga River Crossings — Stalingrad
sub: The Volga River formed the only supply route for Soviet defenders during the Battle of Stalingrad. Under constant German artillery and air attack, supply boats crossed at night maintaining the impossible defense of the city.

label: Prokhorovka — Kursk Salient
sub: Site of the largest tank engagement of Operation Citadel (July 12, 1943). Approximately 800 German and Soviet tanks clashed in a six-hour battle. Soviet forces absorbed the attack and launched their counter-offensive, permanently breaking German offensive power on the Eastern Front.

label: Dieppe, France
sub: Site of the disastrous Allied raid (August 19, 1942) testing amphibious assault techniques. Of 6,000 Allied troops (mostly Canadians), nearly 4,000 were killed, wounded, or captured in under six hours. Lessons from Dieppe directly informed D-Day planning.

label: Casablanca, Morocco
sub: Site of the Allied Casablanca Conference (January 1943) where Roosevelt and Churchill demanded Germany's 'unconditional surrender.' The conference also decided on the invasion of Sicily and agreed to prioritize the U-boat campaign. Stalin was conspicuously absent.

label: Tehran, Iran
sub: Site of the Tehran Conference (November–December 1943) — the first meeting of the 'Big Three' (Roosevelt, Churchill, Stalin). Stalin secured agreement on a definite date for Overlord (D-Day) in exchange for Soviet commitment to a Pacific offensive after Germany's defeat.

label: Caen, Normandy
sub: Key D-Day objective that took seven weeks and heavy bombing to capture (July 19, 1944) rather than D-Day itself. The battle for Caen tied down German panzer forces while US forces broke out at Saint-Lô in Operation Cobra.

label: Maginot Line, France
sub: France's elaborate defensive fortification along the Franco-German border, bypassed entirely by German forces through the Ardennes in May 1940. The Maginot Line became synonymous with a 'defensive mentality' and static thinking in military strategy.

label: Munich
sub: Birthplace of the Nazi Party (NSDAP) and site of the failed Beer Hall Putsch (1923). The Munich Agreement (September 1938) was signed here, allowing Germany to annex the Sudetenland. Liberated by US 7th Army on April 30, 1945.

label: Berchtesgaden — Eagle's Nest
sub: Hitler's mountain retreat in the Bavarian Alps, used for meetings with senior Nazis and foreign dignitaries. The 'Eagle's Nest' (Kehlsteinhaus) was built as a 50th birthday gift for Hitler. Captured intact by US 101st Airborne Division in May 1945.

label: Wolf's Lair, Rastenburg (Kętrzyn)
sub: Hitler's primary Eastern Front headquarters in East Prussia. Site of the July 20, 1944 assassination attempt by Colonel von Stauffenberg. Hitler survived; 4,980 people were arrested in the aftermath and nearly 5,000 executed.

label: Brest Fortress, Belarus
sub: Soviet border fortress that held out against German assault for weeks after Barbarossa began (June 1941). Soviet defenders fought to the last man. The fortress became a symbol of Soviet resistance; survivors were executed as POWs.

label: Omaha Beach
sub: Most heavily defended D-Day landing zone; US 1st and 29th Infantry Divisions suffered 2,000–5,000 casualties against well-positioned German defenses. Despite catastrophic losses, US forces secured the beach by nightfall through individual acts of heroism.

label: Utah Beach
sub: D-Day's westernmost US landing zone; US 4th Infantry Division suffered only 197 casualties — fewest of any beach — due to misnavigation landing in a lightly defended sector. General Theodore Roosevelt Jr. led troops personally; he was the oldest man to land on D-Day.

label: Philippine Sea
sub: Site of the Battle of the Philippine Sea (June 19–20, 1944), the 'Great Marianas Turkey Shoot.' US Navy destroyed 600 Japanese aircraft and sank three carriers, permanently ending Japan's offensive carrier air power.

label: Coral Sea
sub: Site of the first aircraft carrier battle in history (May 4–8, 1942). The US halted Japan's advance toward Australia, sinking the carrier Shōhō. Tactical draw but strategic Allied victory — Japan's Port Moresby invasion was stopped.

label: Peenemünde, Germany
sub: German rocket research and development center on the Baltic coast, where Werner von Braun's team developed the V-1 flying bomb and V-2 ballistic missile. Bombed by RAF in August 1943. The site delayed the V-weapon program by months.

label: Babi Yar, Kyiv
sub: Site of one of WWII's largest single massacres: SS Einsatzgruppen and German police shot 33,771 Jews in two days (September 29–30, 1941). Total executions at Babi Yar exceeded 100,000. The site was suppressed by the Soviet Union; Yevtushenko's 1961 poem brought it to world attention.

label: Chełmno Extermination Camp
sub: First Nazi extermination camp to use gas for mass murder (December 1941). Mobile gas vans were used to murder approximately 340,000 Jews. Operated in two phases (1941–43 and 1944–45), Chełmno was one of the most lethal Operation Reinhard facilities.

label: Ravensbrück Concentration Camp
sub: Principal Nazi camp for women, in northern Germany. Approximately 130,000 women of 30+ nationalities were imprisoned; 50,000–90,000 died of starvation, disease, shooting, or the gas chamber opened in late 1944. Liberated by Soviet forces April 1945.

label: Hong Kong
sub: British crown colony captured by Japan on Christmas Day 1941 (Black Christmas) after 18 days of resistance by British, Canadian, and Hong Kong forces. 12,000 Allied prisoners endured brutal captivity; civilian population suffered widespread atrocities.

label: Manila, Philippines
sub: Philippine capital captured by Japan in January 1942 and liberated by US forces (February–March 1945). The Battle of Manila caused massive civilian casualties estimated at 100,000 deaths — one of WWII's worst urban atrocities, as Japanese forces massacred civilians.

label: Nanjing, China
sub: Site of the Nanjing Massacre (December 1937–January 1938): Japanese forces murdered an estimated 200,000–300,000 Chinese civilians and prisoners of war. The atrocity is also called the 'Rape of Nanking.' China and Japan continue to dispute casualty figures.

label: Ledo/Burma Road
sub: Allied supply route through northern Burma and China, cut by Japan in 1942. The US Army Corps of Engineers built the Ledo Road to restore the supply link to China's Nationalist forces. Completed in January 1945, the route played a key logistical role.

label: Suez Canal
sub: Critical strategic waterway linking the Mediterranean to the Red Sea, controlled by Britain throughout the war. German-Italian advance in North Africa aimed to seize the canal and cut Allied oil supply routes. El Alamein victory saved the canal.

label: Malta
sub: British island fortress in the central Mediterranean under intense German and Italian aerial bombardment (1940–43). Malta's resistance denied Axis forces a vital staging point. King George VI awarded the island the George Cross — the first collective award of the decoration.

label: Crete
sub: Site of the first major airborne invasion (May 1941): Germany deployed 22,000 paratroopers in Operation Mercury to capture the island. Despite heavy German losses among elite paratroopers, Crete fell in 12 days. German losses were so severe they never mounted another major airborne operation.

label: Addis Ababa, Ethiopia
sub: Capital of Ethiopia liberated from Italian occupation by British and Ethiopian forces in April 1941, with Emperor Haile Selassie restored. The East African campaign (1940–41) destroyed Italy's empire in East Africa — one of the first Allied land victories.

label: Cologne, Germany
sub: First German city bombed by 1,000 aircraft in a single night (Operation Millennium, May 30–31, 1942). The raid demonstrated RAF Bomber Command's growing capability; 500 acres of the city were destroyed. First major German city reached and captured by US forces (March 6, 1945).

label: Lübeck/Rostock, Germany
sub: Historic German Hanseatic cities heavily bombed by RAF in early 1942 (first area bombing of a German city). The Lübeck raid (March 28–29, 1942) provoked Hitler to order 'Baedeker Raids' against British cultural cities in retaliation.

label: Minsk, Belorussia
sub: Capital of Soviet Belorussia captured by Germany in just one week (June 28, 1941) during Barbarossa, with 290,000 Soviet prisoners taken. Liberated by Soviet forces on July 3, 1944 during Operation Bagration. 85% of the city was destroyed during German occupation.

label: Kyiv (Kiev), Ukraine
sub: Soviet capital of Ukraine encircled and captured (September 1941) in one of history's greatest military envelopments, yielding 665,000 Soviet prisoners. Recaptured by Soviet forces on November 6, 1943. The Babi Yar massacre occurred in the city's outskirts.

label: Odessa, Ukraine
sub: Soviet Black Sea port besieged by Romanian forces for 73 days (August–October 1941). Heroic Soviet defense before strategic evacuation; 100,000 Jews murdered by Romanian forces during occupation. Liberated by Soviet forces April 10, 1944.
